retrieved_docs.title: Tuberculosis in Children -- History and Physical -- Other disease manifestations
retrieved_docs.content: TB can also present with rare and varied features that are often part of disseminated disease but may occasionally be seen in isolation. Otitis media and mastoiditis [70] [71] , cutaneous lesions called papulonecrotic tuberculids [72] , and ophthalmologic findings of vitritis, keratitis, choroid tuberculids are the result of dissemination of M tuberculosis to distant sites. [73] [74]

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Etiology -- Host Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Contributing host factors to the evolution of OME include:

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Etiology -- Host Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Physiological dysfunction of the Eustachian tube [9] Anatomic blockage Inflammation Allergies Upper respiratory tract infection Trauma

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough medical history should include previous ear infections, hearing loss, tinnitus, vertigo, ear drainage, or prior procedures such as tympanostomy tube placement, tonsillectomy, or adenoidectomy. Pneumatic otoscopy remains essential for evaluating the ear canal and eardrum, including assessment of tympanic membrane mobility and light reflex (see Image . Acute Otitis Media on Otoscopy). When available, binocular microscopes or video-otoscopes provide additional diagnostic support. In some cases, air bubbles, a liquid film, cloudiness, or bluish or yellowish discoloration of the tympanic membrane may be visible.

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Etiology -- Host Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Mucins

retrieved_docs.title: Middle Ear Cholesteatoma -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: When evaluating a patient with suspected middle ear cholesteatoma, it is essential to consider a range of differential diagnoses, as these conditions can present with similar symptoms, such as hearing loss, ear discharge, and chronic ear infections. Key differential diagnoses to consider in such cases include: Acute otitis media Chronic suppurative otitis media Malignant otitis externa Otitis media with effusion Middle ear osteoma Typanosclerosis Tympanic membrane perforation Cholesterol granuloma Granulomatous disease Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (Wegener granulomatosis) Histiocytosis X Mycobacterial infection Primary auricular or temporal bone malignancy (rare)

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Etiology -- Host Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic predisposition

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Etiology -- Host Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Age The eustachian tube is positioned more horizontally in younger children than at a 45-degree angle in adults. As the child develops into an adult, the tube elongates and angles caudally. The size and shape of the Eustachian tube at birth are unfavorable for the ventilation of the middle ear. The peak incidence of OME occurs in children aged 2 to 4 years. [15] Adults with unilateral OME must be evaluated for a nasopharyngeal mass. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: Many children present with a history of prior ear infections or hearing changes. Complaints may include a sensation of blocked ears, popping sounds, ear pain, or frequent ear tugging. Most children experience an episode of acute otitis media—characterized by fever, pain, and upper respiratory symptoms—before developing OME. In adults, OME typically presents unilaterally and often follows upper respiratory symptoms. Adult patients may report hearing loss, tinnitus, or a sensation of fullness or obstruction in the ear canal. Middle ear fluid accumulation in adults often occurs after pressure changes, such as during flying or diving.

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The following factors should be kept in mind when managing OME:

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Children with persistent OME should be evaluated for craniofacial anomalies, congenital disorders, or other systemic etiologies.

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: Hearing loss, though not always readily apparent, represents the most frequently reported symptom of OME by patients, families, or caregivers. OME commonly results in communication difficulties, delayed language development, academic struggles, behavioral or sleep disturbances, social withdrawal, and reduced concentration during activities, eg, conversations, listening to music, or watching television.

retrieved_docs.title: Bezold Abscess -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Because of the advent of antibiotic therapy in the treatment of acute, chronic, and suppurative otitis media, Bezold's abscesses are rarely seen in areas with developed healthcare systems. Still, practitioners should have a high index of suspicion in cases where patients present with a neck mass and clinical signs of previous ear pathology or concurrent ear pathology, particularly in areas of developing healthcare infrastructure or other significant barriers to accessing routine healthcare, or in the immunosuppressed.

retrieved_docs.title: Otitis Media With Effusion -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Antibiotics, steroids, or other medications should be avoided in most patients, particularly without comorbidities.

retrieved_docs.title: Necrotizing (Malignant) Otitis Externa -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with immunosuppression due to HIV or other non-diabetic factors are more likely to develop NOE at a younger age than those with diabetes. [15] Furthermore, individuals with HIV are less prone to Pseudomonas infections but may have a higher risk of fungal infections. In addition, they may not exhibit granulation tissue in the EAC upon physical examination. Generally, these patients experience more unfavorable outcomes than those with diabetes. [16]

Both general pulmonary inflammation and bacterial pneumonia are frequently associated with a positive tuberculin skin test (فحص التوبركولين إيجابي), a finding similarly observed in skull enlargement (حج القحف), suggesting overlapping immunologic or infectious markers. Bacterial pneumonia possesses a transmissible component (přenos), underscoring the need for infection‑control measures when managing affected patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1932
retrieved_docs.content: Myringotomy and drainage of middle ear is identified as a myringotomy, indicating that this procedure involves the incision of the tympanic membrane to allow drainage of fluid from the middle ear.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1952
retrieved_docs.content: Acute bronchitis is classified under the broader category الْتِهابِ شعبي. Clinically, acute bronchitis is associated with الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ. The condition الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ includes a subcategory called التهاب رئوي وليدي. Both الْتِهاب رِئَوِيّ and the measurement حج القحف are linked to a positive فحص التوبركولين, indicating that pulmonary inflammation and head circumference may be related to tuberculin reactivity. This network of relationships highlights the hierarchical classification of respiratory inflammatory disorders and their potential association with immunologic testing results.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2596
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, ophthalmologic and otologic” comprises specific drug combinations used for eye and ear infections, including “dexamethasone and antiinfectives” and “prednisolone and antiinfectives.” Both dexamethasone‑based and prednisolone‑based anti‑infective regimens are members of this combined corticosteroid‑antibiotic category, reflecting their shared role in treating ophthalmic and otologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1684
retrieved_docs.content: Corticosteroids and antiinfectives in combination, otologic is classified within the broader group of otological agents (OTOLOGICALS) and is composed of the specific components hydrocortisone and antiinfectives. This otologic formulation combines a corticosteroid with antimicrobial agents to provide both anti‑inflammatory and anti‑infective effects for ear‑related conditions.

Additionally, head circumference (حج القحف) is clinically associated with a positive tuberculin test, indicating that cranial measurements may provide relevant information in the context of tuberculin screening. Collectively, these relationships portray diabetes mellitus as a pivotal node linking neonatal metabolic disorders, pregnancy complications, drug contraindications, metabolic syndrome, retinal disease, severe limb amputation and bone necrosis, as well as a spectrum of respiratory infections and immunologic testing, all of which are essential considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 837
retrieved_docs.content: Abces van long en mediastinum is classified as a subtype of other lower respiratory disease. The category other lower respiratory disease is associated with خراج (abscess) and is itself a subcategory of اضطراب منصفي. In addition, the imaging finding computed tomography of chest: mediastinal effusion is an instance of اضطراب منصفي. Thus, both the specific clinical entity Abces van long en mediastinum and the radiologic manifestation mediastinal effusion are encompassed within the broader disorder اضطراب منصفي, highlighting the link between lower respiratory infections, abscess formation, and mediastinal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 100
retrieved_docs.content: ear (střední ucho) with otitis media and with بضع طبلة الأذن, illustrating integration of diagnostic and therapeutic pathways. All of these entities are organized under the overarching concept of “Operations by approach,” which classifies each as a form of surgery, thereby providing a coherent hierarchy that links general therapeutic categories to specific surgical and diagnostic interventions, ties each procedure to its anatomical target, and associates those targets with relevant disease entities for precise medical documentation and patient care.
